nativeRange.habitat: Coastal reefs, steep outer reef slopes and drop-offs, typically at depths of 10–60 m, often found in small aggregations in the water column above the reef structure.

diet.notes: Zooplanktivore in the wild. In captivity, have been reported to accept enriched frozen mysis shrimp, frozen brine shrimp, rotifers, calanus, and other small frozen preparations. Vitamin and fatty acid supplementation is generally recommended. Multiple small feedings throughout the day tend to work better than one or two large feedings. Live copepods and amphipods from a refugium have been documented providing valuable supplemental nutrition.

imageCandidates.notes: Photo by John E. Randall, 600x400. Live in-water specimen clearly showing the diagnostic yellow reticulated lines and the small red blood spot on the upper flank; correct ID. Caveat: low resolution and somewhat muted deep-water color.

summary: The Blood Spot Anthias is a colorful reef fish from the Western Pacific, generally considered intermediate in care due to its need for multiple small daily feedings and a stable social group. Best kept as a harem of one male with several females in a spacious reef tank.
careNotes: The Blood Spot Anthias (Pseudanthias luzonensis) is a protogynous hermaphrodite — meaning it starts life as female and can change to male — and this biology shapes how the species is best kept. If the dominant male is lost, the largest female in the group will typically change sex to replace him. Groups of at least six individuals are generally recommended, ideally structured as one male with multiple females; all-female groups will eventually produce their own male through natural sex change. Because males tend to quarrel when more than one is present, keeping only a single male is advised unless the aquarium is very large. The Blood Spot Anthias is also best kept as the sole anthias species in the tank to avoid inter-species competition. These fish are active, open-water swimmers that tend to occupy the middle of the water column, and they appreciate both ample open swimming space and live rock structures to retreat to when threatened. Acclimation can be stressful, and newly acquired specimens have been documented occasionally reverting to accepting only live foods; offering a variety of small frozen foods multiple times daily and maintaining an established refugium that supplies live copepods are commonly recommended practices to ease this transition. The species tends to be susceptible to bacterial and protozoan infections, particularly when stressed during transport.
breedingNotes: The Blood Spot Anthias is rarely if ever bred in captivity. In the wild, the species forms harems in which one dominant male presides over a group of females; if the dominant male is removed, the largest female will typically undergo sex change and assume the male role. No well-documented captive spawning protocols have been established for this species in the hobby.

additional.name: aquaticsunlimited.com
additional.notes: Cross-checked sizing, water parameters, tank size, temperament, and grouping behavior. Agrees on 125 gal / 5 in size / semi-aggressive / groups of 6+. Also notes susceptibility to bacterial/protozoan infection and risk of live-food-only reversion under stress.

additional.name: FishBase
additional.notes: Used for taxonomy (family Anthiadidae, order Perciformes), native range (Western Pacific: Japan south to Australia, east to Vanuatu), and max size confirmation (14.5 cm TL). FishBase temperature range 22–27°C; hobby sources give 22–25.6°C. Final max temperature set to 26°C as average of the three source maxima.

fish.breedingNotes: Rarely if ever bred in captivity. The species is a protogynous hermaphrodite that forms harems with one dominant male; loss of the male triggers sex change in the largest female present.
